steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The claim says Newland compares his intellect 'favorably' to other men. The evidence must show direct intellectual superiority with specific reasons, not just general attitude.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — "Singly [the men around Newland] betrayed their inferiority; but grouped together they represented 'New York,' and the habit of masculine solidarity made him accept their doctrine on all the issues called moral.": No intellectual comparison at all. ✗
  • Choice B — "Newland Archer, leaning against the wall at the back of the club box [where his seat was], turned his eyes from the stage and scanned the opposite side of the house.": Covers manners, not intellectual standing relative to others. ✗
  • Choice C — "Newland Archer felt himself distinctly the superior of these chosen specimens of old New York gentility; he had probably read more, thought more, and even seen a good deal more of the world, than any other man of the number.": Explicitly states intellectual superiority and backs it with concrete evidence ('read more, thought more, and even seen a good deal more of the world'). ✓
  • Choice D — "To come to the Opera in a [carriage for hire] was almost as honourable a way of arriving as in one's own carriage.": Shows deference to collective norms, not a favorable intellectual self-comparison. ✗
C is correct.
